summary: Gruber reviews Amazon's new MP3 Store and finds it to be the most credible competitor to Apple's iTunes Store yet. The store offers completely DRM-free music at 256 kbps with strong major label participation and solid Mac software support. He walks through the purchase and download experience, noting the high 'it just works' factor once the Amazon MP3 Downloader helper app is installed. While Amazon's catalog of two million songs is smaller than iTunes' six million, all of Amazon's tracks are DRM-free, potentially giving them the largest DRM-free library. Gruber argues that Amazon's combination of lower prices, no DRM, and good audio quality makes it the obvious choice over DRM-restricted iTunes purchases.
key_points: Amazon MP3 Downloader integrates seamlessly with iTunes and iPods, automatically importing purchased music with no manual steps required
Amazon offers variable pricing that undercuts iTunes, with singles at $0.89-$0.99 and albums starting at $4.99
While Amazon has fewer total songs than iTunes, all two million are DRM-free, likely giving them more DRM-free tracks than iTunes Plus
The Mac download experience is polished but the installer lacks transparency about what it installs, similar to Google Desktop's installer
Gruber sees no reason to buy DRM-restricted iTunes music when the same songs are available DRM-free and cheaper from Amazon
money_quotes: The Amazon MP3 Store looks like no previous iTunes Store rival.
There's a very high 'it just works' factor here.
They've introduced variable pricing to sell music for less, not more, than the iTunes Store.
All of Amazon MP3's songs are DRM-free.
While Amazon can't claim to offer the most songs, they might be able to claim the most DRM-free songs.
I can't see why anyone would buy DRM-restricted music from iTunes that's available from Amazon.
The Amazon MP3 Store is clearly the biggest and best rival to the iTunes Store. It's not a coincidence that they've eschewed DRM completely.
key_insight: Amazon's MP3 Store succeeds as the first real iTunes rival by making a simple bet: DRM-free music at lower prices with a seamless user experience eliminates any reason to buy restricted tracks from iTunes.
